coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots functionalized polyaniline as efficient sensing platform for cancer biomarker detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chellachamy Anbalagan, Amarnath
Korram, Jyoti
Koyande, Pallavi
Khan, Sheeza
Agrawal, Richa
Sawant, Shilpa N.
coredata.dc:description: Implementing simple and rapid detection of cancer biomarker using biosensors has the potential to contribute greatly to basic biological research and early detection of cancer. Tailoring of biosensor matrix based on conducting polymers has gained significant attention in recent years due to its electroactivity, redox property and biocompatibility. Herein, biomass derived carbon dots (CDs) were anchored on polyaniline (PANI) surface to get biocompatible and fluorescent, carbon dots functionalized polyaniline (CDs@PANI) that enable binding of large amount of prostate cancer biomarker, prostate specific antigen (PSA) antibody on its surface via active functional groups. Immobilization efficiency of CDs@PANI was investigated by electrochemical methods which revealed dense immobilization of biomolecules on CDs@PANI as compared to conventional PANI. A sandwich electrochemical biosensor was constructed using CDs@PANI as immobilization matrix for detection of prostate cancer biomarker PSA. The sensor displayed good sensitivity, specificity, stability, reproducibility, wide linear range (0.01–60 ng/ml) and a low detection limit of 20 pg/ml. The importance of CDs in improving the sensor performance was demonstrated by carrying out detailed electrochemical studies using glucose oxidase as a model biomolecule. Biocompatibility of sensing matrix was assessed by performing 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay for cell viability using WI26 cells. The sensing platform demonstrated herein provides great potential for the detection of different cancer biomarkers and metabolites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyaniline
Carbon dots
Immobilization
Electrochemical biosensor
Cancer biomarker

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Human serum analysis using CDs@PANI based biosensor
Cell viability evaluation by MTT assay
CDs@PANI as sensing matrix: PSA sandwich biosensor
Immobilization efficiency of CDs@PANI sensing matrix
Specificity, stability and reproducibility of PSA immunosensor
Estimation of PSA in real human blood sample and spike-recovery analysis

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) CV curves of PSAAb1 immobilized SPE-CDs@PANI electrode in PBS containing different concentrations of HQ, (b) CV curves of PSAAb2 bound SPE-CDs@PANI electrode in PBS containing 1 mM HQ at different concentrations of H2O2 (c) CV current response of PSAAb2 bound SPE-CDs@PANI electrode at different concentrations of H2O2.
DPV of PSAAb2 modified (a) PANI and (b) CDs@PANI in PBS containing 1 mM HQ with and without H2O2.
CV curves of (a) SPE, (b) SPE-CDs@PANI, (C) SPE-CDs@PANI-PSAAb1(d) SPE-CDs@PANI-PSAAb1-BSA, (e) SPE-CDs@PANI-PSAAb1-BSA-PSA and (f) SPE-CDs@PANI-PSAAb1-BSA-PSA-PSAAb2 in 1 mM HQ.
DPV of sensor electrode without HQ and with HQ, in presence and absence of H2O2.
(a) DPV response of CDs@PANI based PSA biosensor with PSAAb2 detection probe in 1 mM HQ and 2 mM H2O2 with different concentrations of PSA (b) Calibration plot for CDs@PANI based PSA biosensor.

(a) Sensor response in presence of common interferants, (b) Cathodic peak current values obtained from CV of PSAAb1 modified SPE-CDs@PANI in PBS containing 1 mM HQ at 50 mV/s for 100 cycles, (c) Current response of six different sensor electrodes to 50 ng/ml PSA.

CV for (a) Pt-CDs@PANI-GOx in PBS containing different concentration of glucose (b) Current response (Calibration plot) of Pt-CDs@PANI-GOx and Pt-PANI-GOx sensor electrodes with different concentrations of glucose; Calibration plot showing linear range for (c) Pt-CDs@PANI-GOx and (d) Pt-PANI-GOx sensor electrodes, (e) Lineweaver-Burk plot for current response of Pt-CDs@PANI-GOx sensor towards glucose addition.
Construction of PSA immunosensor using CDs@PANI as sensing matrix
Schematic depicting the detection mechanism of PSA in the sandwich electrochemical immunosensor on CDs@PANI and conventional PANI sensing matrix
H2O2 reduction by HRP enzyme in the presence of HQ as mediator

head.ce:title: Carbon dots functionalized polyaniline as efficient sensing platform for cancer biomarker detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai 400085, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai 400085, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Homi Bhabha National Institute, Anushaktinagar, Mumbai 400094, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Homi Bhabha National Institute, Anushaktinagar, Mumbai 400094, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai 400085, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Implementing simple and rapid detection of cancer biomarker using biosensors has the potential to contribute greatly to basic biological research and early detection of cancer. Tailoring of biosensor matrix based on conducting polymers has gained significant attention in recent years due to its electroactivity, redox property and biocompatibility. Herein, biomass derived carbon dots (CDs) were anchored on polyaniline (PANI) surface to get biocompatible and fluorescent, carbon dots functionalized polyaniline (CDs@PANI) that enable binding of large amount of prostate cancer biomarker, prostate specific antigen (PSA) antibody on its surface via active functional groups. Immobilization efficiency of CDs@PANI was investigated by electrochemical methods which revealed dense immobilization of biomolecules on CDs@PANI as compared to conventional PANI. A sandwich electrochemical biosensor was constructed using CDs@PANI as immobilization matrix for detection of prostate cancer biomarker PSA. The sensor displayed good sensitivity, specificity, stability, reproducibility, wide linear range (0.01–60 ng/ml) and a low detection limit of 20 pg/ml. The importance of CDs in improving the sensor performance was demonstrated by carrying out detailed electrochemical studies using glucose oxidase as a model biomolecule. Biocompatibility of sensing matrix was assessed by performing 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay for cell viability using WI26 cells. The sensing platform demonstrated herein provides great potential for the detection of different cancer biomarkers and metabolites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data related to this article has been provided in main mannuscript and supplementarty information

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is one of the most life-threatening diseases and leading cause of death worldwide. The quantification of serum biomarkers is an effective way for cancer detection and to evaluate the effectiveness of treatment. Consequently, this also supports early diagnosis that not only circumvents progression but also reduces economic burden of patients [1]. Biosensors are extensively used in biomedical research and clinical diagnosis as they are capable of detecting a variety of metabolites and cancer biomarkers in physiological samples at nanomolar level [2,3]. Several bioanalytical devices were developed for the detection of cancer biomarkers in serum which includes enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, radio immunoassay, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, surface plasmon resonance (SPR), chemiluminescence, electrochemiluminescent and electrochemical immunoassay. Among these methods, the electrochemical sensors have attracted much attention because of its easy fabrication, simple operation, cost-effectiveness, portability and high sensitivity. Furthermore, electrochemical assays pave way for point-of-care testing systems which has a potential to bypass conventional clinical assays [4].

Recently, there has been immense interest in the development of conducting polymer based matrix for electrochemical sensing applications. Polyaniline (PANI) is one of the most explored conducting polymers in the field of electrochemical biosensors due to its transduction capacity and biocompatibility [5,6]. To improve its analytical performance, PANI based composites have been widely encountered. PANI was integrated with carbon nanotubes (CNT) and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) and used as the sensing platform for the label-free detection of prostate specific antigen (PSA). Inclusion of CNT and Au NPs in the sensing matrix facilitated the immobilization of large amount for capture antibody and rapid electron transfer process which afforded high sensitivity and wide linear range (1.66 ag/ml to 1.3 ng/ml) to the sensor [7]. PANI with Au NPs and peptide were demonstrated for label-free electrochemical PSA biosensor which afforded very low detection limit and wide linear range. Peptide present in the sensor provided antifouling property while Au NPs offered excellent conductivity to sensing platform [8]. Suresh et al. fabricated electrochemical sandwich immunosensor for PSA detection based on PANI, fullerene-C60 and palladium nanoparticles (Pd NPs) nanocomposite as sensing matrix. Sensor afforded high sensitivity due to large surface area, excellent conductivity and electro-catalytic nature of Pd NPs, PANI and fullerene-C60 [9]. Dey et al. demonstrated mediator-free PSA detection using PANI-Au NPs hybrid as the sensing matrix using differential pulse voltammetry. Au NPs present in the sensing matrix facilitated rapid electron transfer, enhanced electroactive surface area and provided suitable micro-environment for capture antibody binding [10].

In our earlier work, a sandwich immunoassay was constructed for detection of liver cancer biomarker alphafeto protein (AFP) in human blood serum using PANI integrated silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) sensing platform (PANI-BSA-Ag) where horseradish peroxidase (HRP) conjugated AFP antibody loaded Ag NPs were used as the detection probe. Ag NPs along with BSA enriched the sensing platform with amine functional groups which facilitated the immobilization of large amount of capture antibody that consequently supported in the widening of linear range of biosensor. Furthermore, detection probe enhanced electrochemical signal during its binding with AFP. Immunoassay exhibited wide linear range with detection limit 4.7 pg/ml [11]. Polyethylenimine (PEI) stabilized PANI NPs was used as the sensing platform for the bimodal detection of PSA. The amine groups present in PEI facilitated the binding of large amount of antibody on its surface which afforded wider linear range for detection of PSA. [12]. Apart from composites, covalently modified PANI has also played key role in enriching sensing matrix with functional groups. Carboxylic acid group enriched PANI was synthesized via thiol-ene click chemistry and used as the generic immobilization matrix for cancer biomarker and metabolites detection. Further, the matrix was used for simultaneous detection of multiple cancer biomarkers (carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and AFP) using redox-labeled detection probes. This sensor could operate in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) as supporting electrolyte without mediator and substrate demonstrating its potential for application in the point-of-care testing systems. [13,14].

Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon based nanomaterials with size <10 nm. Due to unique optical features, size, water solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity and surface functional groups, CDs have drawn a lot of attention as a promising material for sensing, drug delivery and bio-imaging applications [15–20]. In majority of reports, CDs are used for bio-imaging and a very few groups have used it as the probe for cancer biomarker detection in sandwich electrochemical assay. In one of our recent report, bio-functionalized carbon dots (CDs) were used as the detection probe for the sensitive detection of cancer biomarker CEA on PEI grafted PANI based sensing platform [21]. In another work, CDs were used as the probe for multimodal electrochemical and fluorescence immunoassay for the detection of PSA, where a paper based smartphone integrated immunosensor was demonstrated [12].

In the present work, CDs were covalently grafted on conventional PANI (CDs@PANI) and used as the efficient sensing platform for detection of PSA, a prostate cancer biomarker. The sensing platform was characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR), fluorescence spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering and electrochemical analyses. Detection of PSA on CDs@PANI was carried out by differential pulse voltammetry in the presence of redox mediator hydroquinone (HQ) and H2O2. The role of CDs in enhancing the sensor characteristics was studied by using glucose oxidase as model.

Aniline (monomer), ammonium persulfate (oxidant), N-(3-Dimethyl amino propyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS), bovine serum albumin, disodium hydrogen phosphate, monosodium dihydrogen orthophosphate, glucose oxidase (17.3 units per mg, from Aspergillus niger), D-(+)-Glucose, ≥ 99.5 %, hydroquinone and hydrogen peroxide were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Merck) and used as received. Monoclonal PSA antibody (PSAAb1), HRP conjugated polyclonal PSA antibody (PSAAb2) and PSA were procured from Bioss Antibodies, USA. All other reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4) was made by mixing the solutions of dibasic and monobasic sodium phosphate and used as the supporting electrolyte for electrochemical studies and sensor processing.

Modified PANI was characterized by FTIR and fluorescence spectroscopy and electrochemical analyses. ATR-FTIR spectroscopy was carried out using Bruker, ALPHA-II Compact FTIR Spectrometer. The fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded with a spectro-fluorometer (JASCO FP8500). Electrochemical studies like cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were performed in Biologic, France electrochemical work station. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] and K4[Fe(CN)6] (frequency 1 MHz – 1 Hz, amplitude 10 mV, at 0.2 V) using Metrohm Autolab electrochemical work station. Sensor was constructed by drop-casting aqueous CDs@PANI onto a working electrode of screen printed carbon electrode (SPE) followed by drying at 50 °C for 10 min. All the processing steps during sensor construction like antibody immobilization, antigen binding and detection probe binding and sensing experiments were performed in PBS (pH 7.4), which is ideal medium to process biomolecules as cited in several related work [22,23]. All the experiments were performed at ambient temperature [24].

CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using banana petioles extract as reported in our previous work [17]. PANI was synthesized by a conventional oxidative chemical polymerization method similar to reported literature [25]. Firstly, solution containing 0.232 g aniline in 12.5 ml of 1 M HCl was prepared. To this solution, 12.5 ml of ammonium persulfate solution (0.285 g in 1 M HCl) was added drop-wise for 5 min under constant stirring. Reaction was stirred for 4 h at room temperature to produce green PANI salt, which was filtered, washed with 1 M HCl, deionized water and acetone followed by a final wash with deionized water. Thus obtained PANI salt was dispersed in deionized water to obtain a 5 mg/ml stock solution. The PANI suspension was centrifuged and the pellet was dispersed in CDs solution in presence of EDC/NHS and kept undisturbed at 4 °C for 24 h. Then the reaction mixture was centrifuged to remove unbound CDs, unreacted EDC and NHS. The resultant precipitate (CDs@PANI) was used for further studies and characterization. For electrochemical studies, CDs@PANI was drop-casted on SPE and dried at 50 °C for 10 min.

To construct PSA sandwich biosensor (Scheme 1 ), capture antibody, PSAAb1 (500 ng/ml) was first immobilized on the SPE-CDs@PANI via EDC/NHS coupling (0.1 M/0.05 M) for 30 min. The modified electrode was then incubated in BSA solution (1 wt%) for 10 min to block the remaining active –COOH groups to eliminate non-specific binding. Standard solutions of PSA of different concentration were incubated with PSAAb1 bound electrode for 30 min. This was followed by incubation of the electrode with detection probe for 30 min. CV and DPV were recorded for the modified electrode in PBS containing 1 mM HQ and 2 mM H2O2. The current produced on reduction of H2O2 by PSAAb2 bound to PSA was measured. CV was carried out between −0.8 V to 0.8 V Vs Ag/AgCl at the scan rate of 50 mV/s. DPV measurements were carried out at 0.8 V to −0.5 V at pulse height (PH) 5 mV, pulse width (PW) 100 ms, step height (SH) -20 mV and step time (ST) 500 ms. For particular PSA concentration, the difference in value of current for 1 mM HQ + 2 mM H2O2 and blank i.e. 1 mM HQ + 0 mM H2O2 (ΔI = IH2O2 – Iblank) was used to obtain a calibration plot of current difference (ΔI) vs. concentration of PSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Human serum analysis using CDs@PANI based biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Blood sample collected in vial was kept undisturbed for 30 min for coagulation. Then the coagulated portion was separated by centrifuge at 5000 rpm for 8 min. The supernatant was stored at −20 °C for further analysis.

CDs@PANI deposited Pt electrode was covalently modified with glucose oxidase via EDC/NHS coupling for 24 h at 4 °C to construct a glucose biosensor. Analytical performance of glucose biosensor was carried out by CV in PBS containing glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability evaluation by MTT assay
ce:section.ce:para: WI26 cells were seeded in 96-well plate at a seeding density of 1 × 104 cells/well and treated with PANI and CDs@PANI for 24 h. Cells were treated with MTT at a final concentration of 0.5 mg/ml for 4 h in CO2 (5 %) incubator at 37 °C. Thus formed formazan crystals were dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide containing 1 % CH3COOH and the absorbance was recorded at 570 nm.

PANI is one of the best electrochemical sensing platforms due to its functional group, electroactivity and biocompatibility. However, to enhance the immobilization efficiency of PANI, composite preparation and covalent modification approach are frequently used. In the present work, CDs were anchored on PANI to get carboxylic acid functional group enriched sensing platform which can accommodate large amount of capture antibody on its surface. Fig. S1a and b show the FTIR spectra of PANI and CDs@PANI respectively. Spectra displayed the signature peaks of PANI at 1580, 1500, 1302, 1090 and 800 cm−1 which are attributed to quinoid and benzenoid ring stretching vibrations, CN stretching vibrations of aromatic amines, a charged amine structure between the aromatic ring and the 1,4-disubstituted quinoid rings respectively. In addition, a peak at around 1770 and 840 cm−1 was observed for CDs@PANI which is attributed to the CO and CO of carboxylic acid group present on CDs, respectively [15]. The fluorescence spectra of PANI and CDs@PANI (Fig. S2) exhibit emission peaks at 370 nm and 327 nm respectively, with an excitation wavelength of 220 nm (Fig. S2b). PANI maintains a consistent emission peak at 370 nm across different excitation wavelengths, but experiences a decrease in intensity as the excitation wavelength ranges from 200 to 260 nm (Fig. S2a). Conversely, CDs@PANI displays an excitation wavelength-dependent emission peak (Fig. S2b) similar to CDs [17], suggesting the presence of CDs on the surface of PANI. This aligns with the characteristic excitation-dependent fluorescent emission associated with CDs, a notable feature in their spectroscopic behavior. These findings from FTIR and FL spectroscopy confirmed the anchoring of CDs on PANI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs@PANI as sensing matrix: PSA sandwich biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Prostate cancer (PCa) develops in the prostate gland is the second‑leading cause of cancer deaths among men and about 1 out of 9 men are diagnosed with PCa in their lifetime [26]. Early diagnosis of PCa via cancer biomarker detection has significant importance in effective treatment and improving survival chance of patients [27]. PSA, a 34 kDa 240-amino-acid glycoprotein secreted by prostatic epithelial cells is serum based cancer biomarker for PCa [28]. The normal level of PSA in serum is <4 ng/ml and the elevated level higher than 10 ng/ml increase the risk of PCa [29]. A sandwich electrochemical biosensor was constructed for detection of PSA using CDs@PANI as a sensing matrix and HRP conjugated PSA antibody (PSAAb2) as the detection probe (Scheme 1). In this sandwich electrochemical biosensor, hydroquinone was used as the redox mediator and H2O2 was used as the substrate which increases the cathodic current due to its reduction by HRP as witnessed in Scheme 2 .

In order to obtain appropriate electrochemical response, optimization of redox mediator (HQ) and substrate (H2O2) concentration is important. CV of the SPE-CDs@PANI in PBS containing different concentrations of HQ was recorded as shown in Fig. 1a. A well-defined redox peak was observed with 1 mM HQ. Hence this concentration was chosen for the electrochemical experiments. CV was carried out for PSAAb2 bound SPE-CDs@PANI electrode in PBS containing 1 mM HQ with different concentrations of H2O2. HRP present in PSAAb2 reduces H2O2 and for 2 mM H2O2, maximum electro-catalytic reduction current was observed (Fig. 1b and c). Hence, 1 mM HQ and 2 mM H2O2 were used for all electrochemical detection events. Capture antibody immobilization time (30 min), PSA incubation time (30 min), and detection probe incubation time (30 min) were followed as per our earlier report [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization efficiency of CDs@PANI sensing matrix
ce:section.ce:para: EIS is a powerful tool to investigate interfacial properties at electrode surface associated with immobilization of biomolecules. EIS was performed to study the interfacial properties of PSAAb1 bound CDs@PANI and PANI in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] and K4[Fe(CN)6]. Nyquist plot of both the electrodes displayed a squashed semicircle at higher frequency region and a line at lower frequency region. Diameter of semicircle implies the extent of charge transfer resistance (Rct) during the immobilization of biomolecules on electrode surface. Comparison of Nyquist plot before and after PSAAb1 immobilization indicates a substantial increase in the Rct for CDs@PANI (375 Ω) than for PANI electrode (119 Ω) (Fig. S3 and Table S1). This signifies the immobilization of larger amount of PSAAb1on CDs@PANI electrode surface as compared to PANI [14]. To further investigate the immobilization efficiency, PSAAb2 was directly immobilized on CDs@PANI and PANI modified SPE using EDC/NHS coupling. HRP present in the PSAAb2 has ability to reduce H2O2 in presence of redox mediator like HQ (Scheme 3 ) which leads to a change in current that can be monitored by DPV measurement [30].

The electro-catalytic reduction of H2O2 was studied using DPV in PBS containing HQ. Both the modified electrodes displayed an increase in reduction current with addition of H2O2. However, CDs@PANI electrode (Fig. 2a and b) produced 2.8 times higher reduction current (ΔI = 1.09 μA) as compared to conventional PANI (ΔI = 0.39 μA). CDs@PANI accommodated large amount of PSAAb2 due to the presence of large amount of –COOH groups originated from the CDs of the sensing platform. These results emphasize that CDs@PANI could bind larger amount of capture antibody on its surface and could act as the suitable sensing platform for PSA detection [11].

A set of CV experiments was performed in 1 mM of HQ to monitor each step of the sensor construction. Fig. 3 presents the CV curves after each immobilization step of sensor construction. For SPE-CDs@PANI, significantly high peak current was observed than bare SPE indicating the enhanced electroactive surface area after modification of SPE with CDs@PANI sensing matrix. When SPE-CDs@PANI was modified step-wise with PSAAb1, PSA and PSAAb2, the peak current decreased gradually with each immobilizing step. The decrease in peak current is due to blocking of electrode surface by insulating protein molecules that limit the electroactive sites for electron transfer, endorsing the successful assembly of biomolecules and sensor construction [9].

In the sandwich immunosensor, HRP conjugated PSA antibody was used to produce the signal in the presence of H2O2. HRP is also a commonly used enzyme for H2O2 biosensor. It contains heme as a prosthetic group, which is the protein active site along with the heme‑iron Fe(III). However, these active sites of HRP are buried in a thick protein shell which creates large distance between the active sites and electrode surface. Therefore direct electron transfer between HRP and an electrode is difficult [31]. To facilitate the electron transfer, redox mediator like HQ was used to shuttle electrons between the redox centers of HRP and the electrode (Scheme 2).

As seen from the Fig. 4 , no peak was observed for the sandwich immunosensor in the absence of HQ during DPV measurement. Furthermore, addition of H2O2 also did not produce any signal. However, in presence of HQ, a peak was observed at −0.2 V (vs Ag/AgCl) and the peak current increased significantly when H2O2 was added. In this detection event, added H2O2 is reduced to H2O by HRPred catalyst, which in turn gets oxidized to HRPox. Then, the redox mediator HQ reduces HRPox in to its native state HRPred and benzoquinone (BQ), the oxidized form of HQ is formed. Thus, formed BQ reduces at electrode surface leading to peak at reduction potential of HQ. This signifies the importance of using HQ in the sandwich immunosensor for PSA detection.

Under optimized experimental conditions, the current response of the biosensor with different concentrations of PSA was measured using DPV measurement. Fig. 5 displays the analytical performance of immunosensor as a function PSA concentration. As seen in the DPV curves (Fig. 5a), current increased linearly with the increasing concentration of PSA from 0.01 to 60 ng/ml (Fig. 5b). It is to be noted that DPV curves displayed were recorded using different sensor electrodes incubated with various concentration of PSA. Limit of detection (LOD) was determined using the formula
Limit of detetion
LOD
= 3 σ / sensitivity
where σ = Standard deviation value of blank. Linear range observed in the present work is wider than that reported for PANI composite [9] and carbon nanomaterials [32,33] based sensing matrix and less wider than Au nanostructures and PANI NPs based sensing platform [12,34]. This performance is attributed to the presence of -COOH groups on the CDs surface, which provided large number of binding sites for antibody. For this sensor, the limit of detection (LOD) calculated at S/N ratio of 3 was found to be 20 pg/ml which is comparable to the value reported in literature [35–37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, stability and reproducibility of PSA immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In clinical analysis, PSA detection is mostly performed in serum and hence, the effect of interfering species such as L-cysteine, L-arginine, ascorbic acid, uric acid, BSA, fetal bovine serum (FBS), AFP and CEA was investigated. As shown in Fig. 6a, there is negligible effect of interferants on the sensor electrode, whereas target protein PSA showed significant response indicating good specificity of the biosensor towards PSA detection. The stability and reproducibility of the biosensor plays an important role in the clinical analysis of PSA. The stability of capture antibody immobilized CDs@PANI electrode was evaluated by carrying out CV for 100 cycles in PBS containing HQ. Consistent CV response was observed with <2 % decrease in anodic peak current even after 100th cycles (Fig. 6b) indicating the excellent stability of biosensor. Reproducibility of the biosensor was investigated by constructing five different sensor electrodes to measure 50 ng/ml PSA. As seen from the Fig. 6c, consistent response was witnessed for all the sensor electrodes with a relative standard deviation (RSD %) of 2.5 %, indicating that the fabrication method was reproducible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of PSA in real human blood sample and spike-recovery analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the sensor for clinical application, detection of PSA in human blood serum sample was carried out using CDs@PANI based biosensor. The concentration of PSA in human serum sample was found to be 0.706 ng/ml which is consistent with the value obtained with the conventional chemiluminescent immunoassay emphasizing the utility of proposed biosensor for clinical analysis. RSD % between the value obtained from these two assays was found to be 3.7 %. Sensor was also evaluated by spike-recovery analysis in which a known concentration of PSA standard (0, 5, 10, 25, 45 ng/ml of PSA) was spiked in the undiluted real human serum sample and incubated with capture antibody bound sensor electrode. Then electrode was incubated with detection probe and DPV measurement was carried out. The result showed that the recovery was 105.3 %, 101.5 %, 99.8 %, 103.8 and 100.5 % (Table S2) for PSA respectively, indicating its potential for application in clinical analysis.

Further, to demonstrate the importance of CDs in improving the linear range and the overall biosensor performance, enzyme glucose oxidase (GOx) was used as a model biorecognition unit to investigate the Michaelis- Menten constant (Km) for the sensing system. Km value is vital parameter of the biosensor which signifies the affinity of enzyme towards corresponding substrate. In our earlier work, antibody was mimicked with HRP enzyme and, H2O2 biosensor was constructed to obtain the Km value [12]. In the present work, glucose biosensor was constructed by immobilizing GOx on CDs@PANI modified Pt electrode. Analytical performance of glucose biosensor was evaluated by carrying our CV measurements for Pt-CDs@PANI-GOx electrode in PBS at different concentrations of glucose. CV curves displayed an increase in oxidation current from 0.55 V to 1 V (Fig. 7a) which is attributed to oxidation of H2O2 (Eq. (2)) released as a result of the reaction between GOx and glucose (Eq. (1)).

The results for glucose biosensors which was constructed using CDs@PANI and PANI based sensing matrix are summarized in Table S3 of ESI. CDs@PANI based glucose biosensor showed higher sensitivity (36.8 μA mM−1 cm−2) with wide linear range (0.5–10 mM) and low detection limit (9 μM) as compared to the biosensor based on PANI based sensing matrix (Fig. 7b, c). CDs provided large number of carboxylic acid groups on its surface which facilitated the immobilization of higher amount of GOx on the sensing matrix leading the wider linear range. The CV current response of the GOx modified electrode towards glucose is shown in Fig. 7b-d. The response plot follows a typical Michaelis-Menten behavior showing linearity in initial stage and saturation at higher glucose concentration. A linear relationship was observed between the reciprocal of current response (1/I) and the reciprocal of glucose concentration (1/C) in the lower concentration range (Fig. 7e) and its slope was used to evaluate Km using the Lineweaver–Burk equation. The value of Km was found to be 2.9 mM, which is lower than that for free GOx system and also lower than the values reported in literature for some of the glucose biosensors [38–41]. These findings emphasize that the presence of CDs in the CDs@PANI based matrix leads to improved biomolecular immobilization efficiency and is a suitable sensing platform for electrochemical biomarker detection.

Cell viability is the vital parameter of biocompatibility, which determines the capability of cells to endure in the presence of foreign materials. In the present study, cell viability measurements were performed to assess the biocompatibility of CDs@PANI from the perspective of possible in-vivo bio-analytical applications. Moreover, biocompatibility is an important parameter for implantable and wearable healthcare devices. From this point of view, WI26 cells were treated with 25–100 μg/ml of CDs, PANI and CDs@PANI for 24 h as described in the experimental section. The percentage of viable WI26 cells with 25, 50 and 100 μg/ml of CDs@PANI is 91.5, 89.5 and 80.4 % respectively (Fig. S4). Even at higher concentration (100 μg/ml) CDs@PANI retained 80.3 % cell viability. As per ISO 10993-5 standard, cell viability values >80 % are considered as non-cytotoxicity; 80–60 % weak; 60–40 % moderate and < 40 % strong cytotoxicity, respectively [42]. Therefore, it is observed that even at higher concentrations of CDs@PANI, WI26 cells were viable indicating the biocompatibility of sensing matrix for potential in vivo bio-analytical applications.

CDs@PANI was synthesized by covalently anchoring of biomass derived CDs on PANI via EDC/NHS coupling and used as the sensing matrix for PSA detection. CDs afforded carboxylic acid groups which facilitated the immobilization of large amount of capture antibody on the sensing matrix. Enhanced immobilization efficiency of sensing matrix was confirmed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and CV studies which corroborated the efficient binding of large amount of capture of antibody on CDs@PANI than conventional PANI based sensing matrix. Sandwich electrochemical immunosensor constructed using CDs@PANI showed wide linear range (0.01 to 60 ng/ml), low limit of detection (20 pg/ml) for PSA detection. Furthermore, Km value obtained for CDs@PANI using glucose as a model biorecognition unit revealed the role of CDs in enhancing analytical performance of sensing matrix. Cell viability evaluation in WI26 cells revealed the biocompatible nature of CDs@PANI for in vivo bio-analytical applications. The present work demonstrates sandwich electrochemical immunosensor for detection of PSA as a model biomarker, however the methodology demonstrated herein can be adopted for determination of a wide range of disease biomarkers and physiologically important metabolites.
The authors have not used any artificial intelligence (AI) or AI-assisted technologies in the writing process.
The authors declare that work described has not been published previously, that it is not under consideration for publication elsewhere, that its publication is approved by all authors.

Amarnath Chellachamy Anbalagan: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Jyoti Korram: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Pallavi Koyande: Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation. Sheeza Khan: Investigation, Validation. Richa Agrawal: Investigation, Validation. Shilpa N. Sawant: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111276.
